content.text:
Review the specification in @specs/lex-vietnamese-telex-input-method.md especially "Tone override rules" to find any gaps, contradictions...

Don't change any content.

Discuss with me to improvem the specification.

content.thinking: I need to review the specification file to identify any gaps or contradictions, particularly in the tone override rules section.

result.content:
1: # Lex - Vietnamese Telex input method
2: 
3: The specification of the Lex program, a Vietnamese Telex input method.
4: 
5: ## Vietnamese Telex Rules
6: 
7: ### Diacritics
8: 
9: Diacritics let a character has many variant. We only focus on character diacritics in this section.
10: Tones have seperated section.
11: 
12: #### Empty
13: 
14: - Any literal alphabet character has empty diacritic by default.
15: - Only a specific number of characters have other diacritic and it must follow the rules for those
16:   characters.
17: 
18: #### Circumflex
19: 
20: - Valid for: `A` (Â), `E` (Ê), `O` (Ô) and their lower cases.
21: 
22: #### Breve
23: 
24: - Valid for: `A` (Ă) and its lower case.
25: 
26: #### Horn
27: 
28: - Valid for: `O` (Ơ), `U` (Ư) and their lower cases.
29: 
30: #### Stroke
31: 
32: - Valid for: `D` (Đ) and its lower case.
33: 
34: #### Apply rules
35: 
36: - Only apply diacritic when the immediate previous character doesn't have any diacritic (empty).
37: - _Circumflex_, _stroke_ are triggered when user inputs the same character (ignore case) with the
38:   existing character when that character is valid for the applying diacritic.
39:   - `Aa` -> `Â`, `Ee` -> `Ê`, `Oo` -> `Ô`.
40:   - `Dd` -> `Đ`.
41: - _Breve_, _horn_ are triggered when user input `W`, `w` after the character when that character is
42:   valid for the applying diacritic. Otherwise, treat `W`, `w` literally.
43:   - `Aw` -> `Ă`.
44:   - `Ow` -> `Ơ`, `Uw` -> `Ư`.
45: - The existing character keep its case, tone.
46: - Treat out of scope characters as literal.
47: 
48: #### Override rules
49: 
50: - New valid diacritic can override existing valid diacritic of the same base character.
51:   - `Ăa` -> `Â`, `Âw` -> `Ă`.
52:   - `Ôw` -> `Ơ`, `Ơo` -> `Ô`.
53: - Only `A`, `O` have override rules because they have more than one possible character diacritic.
54: - Keep tone untouched.
55: - The existing character keep its case, tone.
56: - Treat out of scope characters as literal.
57: 
58: #### Cancel rules
59: 
60: - The cancellation is triggered if and only if user inputs the character (ignore case) that would
61:   produce the existing diacritic for the same base character of the immediate previous character.
62: - The existing character keeps its case, tone, but the diacritic is removed.
63:   - Circumflex: `Âa` -> `Aa`, `Ốo` -> `Óo`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
64:   - Breve: `Ăw` -> `Aw`.
65:   - Horn: `Ưw` -> `Uw`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
66:   - Stroke: `Đd` -> `Dd`, `đD` -> `dD`.
67: - The new character is literal, keeps its case, no tone.
68: - Switch to literal from the new character position and the following characters. Literal input
69:   remains until the new word.
70:   - Existing `Â`, input `aa`, result `Aaa`.
71: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
72: 
73: #### Auto-fill rules
74: 
75: - The auto-fill is only applied for `ươ` syllable immediately before the user input, when one of the
76:   character in the syllable is missing horn: `uơ` and `ưo`.
77: - Fill the missing _horn_ when the input is one of these character (ignore case): `C`, `I`, `M`,
78:   `N`, `P`, `T`, `U` and their lower cases.
79:   - Fill missing horn on `U`: `uơn` -> `ươn`.
80:   - Fill missing horn on `O`: `ưóp` -> `ướp`.
81: - The new input character is appended literally.
82: - Tone and case of existing characters are preserved on the original characters.
83: 
84: ### Tones
85: 
86: Tones let a word has many variants, each variant has different meaning. Only the following
87: Vietnamese vowel could have tones: `A`, `E`, `I`, `O`, `U`, `Y` and their corresponding variants.
88: These vowel could take any tone.
89: 
90: #### Level (ngang)
91: 
92: - The default tone, it's the vowel without any tone mark, e.g. `A`, `Ê`, `Ơ`...and all other
93:   corresponding variants.
94: - If a vowel has tone mark other than level, use `Z`, `z` to reset the tone to level.
95: 
96: #### Rising (sắc)
97: 
98: - Example of rising tone: `Á`, `Ế`, `Ớ`...and all other corresponding variants.
99: - Use `S`, `s` to put the rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `As` -> `Á`, `Ês` -> `Ế`, `Ơs`
100:   -> `Ớ`.
101: 
102: #### Falling (huyền)
103: 
104: - Example of falling tone: `À`, `Ề`, `Ờ`...and all other corresponding variants.
105: - Use `F`, `f` to put the falling tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Af` -> `À`, `Êf` -> `Ề`, `Ơf`
106:   -> `Ờ`.
107: 
108: #### Dipping-rising (hỏi)
109: 
110: - Example of dipping-rising tone: `Ả`, `Ể`, `Ở`...and all other corresponding variants.
111: - Use `R`, `r` to put the dipping-rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ar` -> `Ả`, `Êr` ->
112:   `Ể`, `Ơr` -> `Ở`.
113: 
114: #### Rising-glottalized (ngã)
115: 
116: - Example of rising-glottalized tone: `Ã`, `Ễ`, `Ỡ`...and all other corresponding variants.
117: - Use `X`, `x` to put the rising-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ax` -> `Ã`, `Êx`
118:   -> `Ễ`, `Ơx` -> `Ỡ`.
119: 
120: #### Falling-glottalized (nặng)
121: 
122: - Example of falling-glottalized tone: `Ạ`, `Ệ`, `Ợ`...and all other corresponding variants.
123: - Use `J`, `j` to put the falling-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Aj` -> `Ạ`, `Êj`
124:   -> `Ệ`, `Ơj` -> `Ợ`.
125: 
126: #### Apply rules
127: 
128: - Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
129:   character literally.
130: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
131:   their lower cases.
132: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
133: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, append new character literally.
134: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowels and all of them don't have any tone (level), use _tone
135:   position rules_ to apply the tone for the word.
136: - If _tone position rules_ could not put tone to the pseudo-word, append new character literally.
137: 
138: #### Cancel rules
139: 
140: - Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
141:   character literally.
142: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
143:   their lower cases.
144: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
145: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
146: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowels and has the tone (other than level) on vowels match
147:   with the triggered tone. Reset that tone to level. Append new character literally.
148: - If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
149:   position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
150: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
151: 
152: #### Reset rules
153: 
154: - Only trigger reset tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
155:   character literally.
156: - Trigger reset tone when user inputs the trigger character for reset: `Z`, `z`.
157: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
158: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowels and has tone (other than level) in one of the vowels,
159:   change that tone to level, keep case and diacritic.
160: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels or has at least one vowels and all of them don't have
161:   any tone (level), append the new character literally.
162: - If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
163:   position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
164: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
165: 
166: #### Override rules
167: 
168: - Only trigger override tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
169:   character literally.
170: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
171:   their lower cases.
172: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
173: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
174: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowels and has the tone (other than level) on vowels different
175:   from the triggered tone, reset that tone to level, use _tone position rules_ to apply new tone for
176:   the word.
177: 
178: #### Scan pseudo-word
179: 
180: - Scan pseudo-word is a process to get the information needed from the existing characters to put
181:   the tone in the correct position.
182: - The existing characters must not be empty.
183: - Iterate backward from the last existing character (the new input is the tone trigger character).
184: - If a vowel is found first, continue go backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no
185:   character to scan, then stop.
186: - If a consonant is found first, continue go backward until found the first vowel, then continue go
187:   backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no character to scan, then stop.
188: - After iterated, a context have been formed either (in the left to right order, not the reverse)
189:   `<consonants>`, `<vowels><consonants>`, `<consonant><vowels><consonants>`, `<vowels>` or
190:   `<consonant><vowels>`.
191: 
192: #### Tone position rules
193: 
194: The process of applying tone to a word go through many steps:
195: 
196: - Focus on the `<vowels>` in peusdo-word, it could be one vowel but could be multiple vowels, the
197:   tone will be applied on one of these vowels.
198: - Exceptions:
199:   - Word exactly `QU` (and their lower cases), could not put tone, this is a consonant only.
200: - If one vowel, put the tone on that vowel.
201: - If multiple vowels, follow these rules, priority from top down:
202:   - Vowels has `Ơ` (and its lower case), put on `Ơ`.
203:   - Vowels has one any of `Ê`, `Â`, `Ô`, `Ă`, `Ư` (and their lower cases), put on the listed vowels,
204:     prioritize the first to the last in the list.
205:   - Word start with `GI`, `QU` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels, put on next vowel, `I` /
206:     `U` count as part of the consonant.
207:   - Vowels exactly `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases), put on first vowel.
208:   - Vowels start with `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels or
209:     consonants, put on second vowel.
210:   - Otherwise, put on first vowel.
211: - Preserve case and diacritic on existing vowels.
212: - Could not put tone for other cases.

content.thinking:
I'm reviewing the tone override rules section and noticing a couple of issues—there's what looks like a copy-paste error where "Trigger apply tone" appears in a section about overrides, and I'm seeing a gap in the logic for when a pseudo-word's tone matches the triggered tone exactly.

The bigger problem is that the spec has separate Apply, Cancel, and Override sections all triggered by the same characters (S/F/R/X/J), but it doesn't specify a dispatch order or precedence—how does the system decide which rule to apply first? And the override rules only document the "no vowels" and "different tone" branches, leaving out what happens when tones are equal or when the tone position rules can't place the tone after resetting. Unlike the cancel and reset rules, override doesn't mention falling back to literal mode if it fails.

Looking at the diacritic rules, there's a direct contradiction: line 36 says "only apply diacritic when the previous character has no diacritic," but the override rules at line 51 show `Ăa -> Â` and `Âw -> Ă`, which violate that constraint. The override rules implicitly relax this restriction but never state it explicitly. The auto-fill rules also have a gap—they only handle cases where one character is missing horn, but what if both are missing, like `uo`? That's not covered.

For tone positioning, there's an inconsistency with QU: line 192 says it's consonant-only and can't take tone, but line 205 says if QU starts a word with more vowels, put the tone on the next vowel like in `QUA`. The vowel pair rules also leave gaps—`IA`, `UA`, and `UO` without horn aren't explicitly addressed, though line 210's fallback to "first vowel" would catch them.

The "scan pseudo-word" logic going backward from the end to find context is clear for mixed consonant-vowel patterns, but there's ambiguity when iterating backward finds no vowel at all—the spec doesn't explicitly say the iteration stops at the word boundary, it just implies it.

The bigger gaps are around word boundaries themselves: the spec mentions "new word" multiple times but never defines what constitutes one. Is it whitespace, punctuation, or any non-alphabetic character? And literal mode compounds this—after typing a literal sequence like `Aaa`, if the user types `s`, should that be literal too or apply tone rules? The spec says literal input continues until a new word, but that hinges on undefined word boundary semantics.

There's also a missing case in the tone override logic: what happens when the pseudo-word already has the same tone as the triggered tone? That should cancel the override, but the spec treats cancel and override as separate sections without clarifying how to dispatch between them.

content.text:
I read [specs/lex-vietnamese-telex-input-method.md](file:///<redacted>/lex/specs/lex-vietnamese-telex-input-method.md). Here are the gaps and contradictions I found, ordered by severity.

## Critical: Tone Override / Apply / Cancel dispatch is undefined

The three sections — Apply (L126), Cancel (L138), Override (L166) — all declare the same trigger: "Trigger apply tone when user inputs `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J`...". There is no top-level dispatcher that selects which rule fires. The reader has to infer from the body conditions:

- Apply: pseudo-word vowels are all level.
- Cancel: existing tone == triggered tone.
- Override: existing tone != triggered tone.

This works only if the reader composes them, but the spec never states this precedence or that the three are mutually exclusive branches of a single decision. The Override section header even says "Trigger apply tone" (L170), which reads like a copy-paste leftover and worsens the ambiguity.

Suggestion: either add a single "Tone trigger dispatch" section that states the decision tree, or label Apply/Cancel/Override as branches of one rule.

## Gap: Override rules omit the "could not place tone" outcome

Override (L174–176) says: reset existing tone to level, then run tone position rules. But:

- It does not state what happens when tone position rules cannot place the new tone (Apply explicitly handles this at L136 with "append literally").
- If reset already happened and placement then fails, the word is left half-mutated (existing tone gone, new tone not placed). Spec is silent.
- Override has no literal-switch clause (Cancel L148–150 and Reset L162–164 have one). Should override-failure also switch to literal?

## Gap: Override does not cover the "no vowels" branch symmetrically

Override L173 delegates the "no vowels" case back to Apply rules, which is fine. But it does not delegate the "all vowels level" case — that case is silently the Apply path again. Spec should make this explicit, otherwise a strict reader might conclude "no rule matches" and do nothing.

## Contradiction: Diacritic Apply vs. Diacritic Override

L36 (Diacritic Apply): "Only apply diacritic when the immediate previous character doesn't have any diacritic (empty)."

L50–52 (Diacritic Override): `Ăa -> Â`, `Âw -> Ă`, `Ôw -> Ơ`, `Ơo -> Ô`. These all violate L36 (the previous character already has a diacritic). The Override section implicitly relaxes L36 but never says "L36 does not apply when an override is triggered". A first-time reader will see a contradiction.

## Gap: Diacritic Cancel vs. Override boundary

Both Cancel (L58) and Override (L48) can apply on the same trigger pattern over a vowel that already carries a diacritic. Example with `Â` + `a`:

- Cancel says: same character that produced existing diacritic ⇒ remove (L60–63: `Âa -> Aa`).
- Override says: a *different* valid diacritic overrides. `a` is the same trigger, so Cancel wins.

But the spec never explicitly says "Cancel takes precedence over Override". By construction the trigger sets are disjoint (`a` cancels Â; `w` would override Â to Ă), but again it is not stated.

## Gap: "Word" / "syllable" boundary is undefined

L69, L150, L164 mention "until the new word", and the auto-fill rule talks about "syllable" (L75). The spec never defines what terminates a word: whitespace? punctuation? any non-alpha? digits? This affects every literal-mode exit, every pseudo-word scan, and the auto-fill scope.

## Gap: Pseudo-word scan termination when no vowel exists

L186–187 say "if a consonant is found first, continue go backward until found the first vowel, then continue backward until... first consonant or no character to scan". It does not specify what happens if no vowel is ever found while scanning backward. L189 lists `<consonants>` as a possible result, so presumably the scan stops at buffer/word start, but this is implicit.

## Gap: Auto-fill does not cover `uo` (both missing horn)

L75–76: "one of the character in the syllable is missing horn: `uơ` and `ưo`". A buffer ending in plain `uo` (both empty) is not addressed. Probably intentional (cannot disambiguate), but should be stated.

## Gap: Auto-fill character set is asserted, not derived

L77–78 lists `C, I, M, N, P, T, U` as triggers. There is no rationale (presumably consonants that legally follow `ươ`-syllable, plus `i`/`u` for diphthongs). Without a rationale or a complete enumeration test, future maintainers cannot tell whether the list is exhaustive or arbitrary.

## Minor: Tone position rules — interaction of L203 and L207

L203 (one of `Ê Â Ô Ă Ư`) and L207 (vowels exactly `OA OE OO UY`) can overlap once diacritics are applied. Example: vowels `ÔA`. L207 requires "exactly `OA`" so `ÔA` does not match L207, and L203 catches `Ô`. Consistent, but worth a sentence: "L207/L208 apply only when the listed vowels carry no diacritic." Same with `UY` vs. `Ư` etc.

## Minor: Reset rule (`Z`/`z`) and the no-tone case

L160–161: if no vowels, or all vowels level, append `z` literally and switch to literal mode. This means typing `z` mid-word almost always poisons the rest of the word with literal mode. Intentional? Probably yes (matches Telex behavior), but worth confirming with an example.

## Minor wording

- L9: "let a character has many variant" → "have many variants".
- L10: "Tones have seperated section" → "Tones have a separate section".
- L88: "These vowel could take any tone" → "These vowels".
- L134, L146, L158: "has at least one vowels" → "one vowel" or "at least one vowel".
- L170: header says "Trigger apply tone" but the section is Override.

---

Which of these would you like to tighten first? My recommendation: start with the **tone trigger dispatch precedence** (single section that defines Apply/Cancel/Override/Reset as branches of one decision) and the **diacritic Apply-vs-Override contradiction** (L36 vs L50). Those are the two that change reader understanding the most.
